notes.anchor: on the seventh day before the Kalends
notes.note: Roman dates were counted backward (inclusively) from three fixed monthly points: the Kalends (1st), Nones (5th or 7th), and Ides (13th or 15th). "The seventh day before the Kalends" thus falls in the second half of the preceding month.

notes.anchor: at the Alban estate with Curio
notes.note: The Ager Albanus, the district around the Alban hills southeast of Rome; Caesar broke his march there in late March 49 BC in the company of C. Scribonius Curio, the tribune who had crossed to his side and now served as one of his lieutenants.

notes.anchor: posted one legion each at Brundisium, Tarentum, and Sipontum
notes.note: Sipontum, a port below the Garganus spur of Apulia; Caesar garrisoned it with Brundisium and Tarentum to seal the Adriatic exits of Italy after Pompey's escape.

The college of augurs, the priestly board that interpreted the auspices; their decree could be needed to validate the appointment of magistrates. Cicero, himself an augur, fears Caesar will press the college into legitimizing irregular elections.

The dictatorship was an emergency magistracy of supreme power, traditionally named by a consul; lacking lawful consuls, Caesar would have to resort (as Cicero notes Sulla did) to an interrex naming him. The "master of the horse" (magister equitum) was the dictator's appointed deputy.

notes.anchor: like Quintus Mucius, or, at that man's, like Lucius Scipio
notes.note: Two casualties of the Sullan-Marian terror of 82 BC offered as Cicero's grim alternatives: Q. Mucius Scaevola the Pontifex, murdered by the Marians, and L. Cornelius Scipio Asiaticus (cos. 83), driven out by Sulla. Cicero foresees being destroyed by one side or the other.

Cicero had been hailed imperator by his troops for a minor campaign in Cilicia (51 BC) and still held the title while awaiting a triumph; Matius and Trebatius address him by it as a courtesy. The title constrained him, since an imperator could not lawfully enter the city.
